coredata.dc:title: Photodegradation of ibuprofen using 5-10-15-20-tetrakis(4-bromophenyl) porphyrin conjugated to graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Magaela, Bridged N.
Ndlovu, Knowledge S.
Tshangana, Charmaine S.
Muleja, Adolph A.
Mamba, Bhekie B.
Nyokong, Tebello
Managa, Muthumuni
coredata.dc:description: Ibuprofen (IBU) is a common anti-inflammatory drug that is consumed by many individuals in the world. As such, analytical studies have detected high concentrations of the drug in many waterbodies, which poses a risk of harmful effects on the environment and public health. The hydroxyl radical technologies, a collective of techniques also known as advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), can be utilized to degrade this emerging pollutant. In this study, the photodegradation of ibuprofen using 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-bromophenyl) porphyrin conjugated to graphene quantum dots was investigated using a custom-built photoreactor. Three different concentrations of IBU (200, 300 and 500 μM) were utilized as initial concentrations. The pH of the IBU was varied between acidic (pH 3.0), natural (pH 5.0) and alkaline (pH 9.0) to note the effect on IBU degradation as a function of time. The Highest ФΔ was obtained for InTBrP- GDQs (ФΔ = 0.80), followed by InTBrP (ФΔ = 0.74). The photodegradation efficiency of the TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs were determined to be 43.2 and 76.1% respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysts
Nanoconjugates
Ibuprofen
Pharmaceuticals
Aqueous solution
Degradation mechanisms

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of photocatalyst loading and initial IBU concentration
Identifying radicals responsible for the degradation of IBU

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: College of Science, Engineering and Technology
Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability, University of South Africa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DSI/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University. Funding is also from the Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS) at the College of Science, Engineering and Technology (CSET), University of South Africa.

This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation (NRF) , South Africa through DSI/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology ( UID 62620 ), Rhodes University . Funding is also from the Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS) at the College of Science, Engineering and Technology (CSET), University of South Africa .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible absorption and MCD spectra of photocatalysts (a) TBrP-GQDs and (b) InTBrP-GQDs in DMF.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) distribution curve for photocatalysts TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs.
Fluorescence decay (blue), χ2 ﬁtting (black) and IRF (red) curves for InTBrP-GQDs as an example.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of IBU using TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs photocatalysts and (b) the corresponding degradation constant.
(a) Effect of photocatalyst loading on the degradation of IBU using InTBrP-GQDs (insert corresponding degradation rate), (b) TOC profile of the IBU degradation by InTBrP-GQDs.
Effect of initial IBU concentrations on the photodegradation efficiencies of InTBrP-GQDs.
Effect of initial IBU pH on the photodegradation efficiencies of InTBrP-GQDs.
Quenching experiments on the photodegradation of the IBU.
LC-MS-QTOF degradation products of IBU using InTBrP-GQDs.
Reusability experiments using the InTBrP-GQDs photocatalyst.
The photophysical parameters for TBrP, InTBrP, TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs in DMF unless specified.
Maximum IBU photodegradation, apparent pseudo-first order apparent reaction rate constants and R2 values at varying IBU concentrations using InTBrP-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Photodegradation of ibuprofen using 5-10-15-20-tetrakis(4-bromophenyl) porphyrin conjugated to graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda, 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University. Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS), Florida Campus, College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Johannesburg, 1710, South Africa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS), Florida Campus, College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Johannesburg 1710, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ibuprofen (IBU) is a common anti-inflammatory drug that is consumed by many individuals in the world. As such, analytical studies have detected high concentrations of the drug in many waterbodies, which poses a risk of harmful effects on the environment and public health. The hydroxyl radical technologies, a collective of techniques also known as advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), can be utilized to degrade this emerging pollutant. In this study, the photodegradation of ibuprofen using 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-bromophenyl) porphyrin conjugated to graphene quantum dots was investigated using a custom-built photoreactor. Three different concentrations of IBU (200, 300 and 500 μM) were utilized as initial concentrations. The pH of the IBU was varied between acidic (pH 3.0), natural (pH 5.0) and alkaline (pH 9.0) to note the effect on IBU degradation as a function of time. The Highest ФΔ was obtained for InTBrP- GDQs (ФΔ = 0.80), followed by InTBrP (ФΔ = 0.74). The photodegradation efficiency of the TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs were determined to be 43.2 and 76.1% respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data have been included in the manuscript and supplementary document

ce:section.ce:para:
Over the last few years there has been a growing concern of emerging contaminants (ECs) being detected in surface, groundwater, wastewater effluents globally [1–3]. This is due to the inability of conventional wastewater treatment plants to effectively remove these ECs. Currently, to remove ECs from various water matrices techniques such as membrane technology, activated carbon adsorption, biological processes as well as advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been employed [4]. Of these techniques, AOPs is the most promising and is based on the in-situ generation of radicals such as hydroxyl (·OH) and sulphate (SO4
·-) and typically utilizes light irradiation. In most AOPs applications semiconducting materials such as TiO2 and ZnO have been extensively used, and recently the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in AOPs applications has gained a lot of traction [5].

In this study, the use of metalloporphyrins (MPs) as photocatalysts in the degradation of ECs is investigated. Metalloporphyrins are biomimetic photocatalysts that possess large π-electron systems which results in high absorption in the visible light region and have been investigated in oxidation reactions [6–9]. The drawbacks of using metalloporphyrins as homogeneous photocatalysts include difficulty in recovery and reuse, intermolecular self-oxidation, dimerization, or decomposition during the reaction and are easily deactivated [10–12]. To mitigate some of these limitations, metalloporphyrins can be immobilized on a solid surface; in the current study, we propose the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as solid support to enhance both the photophysical and photocatalytic performance of the metalloporphyrins.

The 5,10,15, 20-tetrakis(4-bromophenyl) metalloporphyrins employed in this work is conjugated to the GQDs via non-covalent π-π interactions. The non-covalent interactions between GQDs and porphyrins undergoes rapid electron transfer. An indium-metalated derivative of the porphyrin was deployed because the presence of the heavy indium metal promotes intersystem crossing to the triplet state where singlet oxygen is generated [13]. Previously, Managa et al. [14] used a similar approach to immobilize pyrene-derivatized porphyrins on GQDs. While Lu et al. [12] functionalized GQDs with a zinc porphyrin (GQDs/ZnP) and used it as a photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue. In this work, the target pollutant is Ibuprofen (IBU); which is an analgesic, anti-inflammatory and antipyretic drug that has been detected in several South African waters [15]. Literature shows that upon excretion about 15% of IBU parent compound is released into the environment unaltered, while 26% is metabolized hydroxyl-IBU and 43% is carboxy-IBU from the ingested dosage.

Recent data show a maximum concentration of 19.2 μg/L for IBU detected in river water in the KwaZulu-Natal Province [16]. In the same province, another study reported a high concentration of 12.94 μg/L for IBU in wastewater effluents suggesting the inefficacy of wastewater treatment plants in removing IBU [17]. Similarly, a maximum concentration of 13.7 μg/L for IBU was detected in a wastewater effluent in the North-West Province [18]. It is therefore necessary to find techniques that can remove IBU as it has been shown that the presence of IBU can have detrimental effects on human and ecosystem even at low concentrations [19].

Among various treatment techniques currently employed, adsorption of IBU using activated carbon is well efficient [20,21]. This is attributed to high binding energy through π-π interactions on the surface of the activated carbon and the solute. IBU is a dipolar compound, its hydrophobicity also promotes adsorption on the smaller pores of the activated carbon [22]. However, disadvantages of activated carbon include the necessity of secondary processes such as disposal, replacement or reactivation. This poses secondary pollution because adsorption separates IBU from the water but does not degrade it. This study focuses on a one-way process for degrading IBU to less harmful products.

To enhance the photocatalytic performance of the 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-bromophenyl) porphyrin (TBrP) and metalated derivative (InTBrP) for the degradation of IBU, GQDs were conjugated to porphyrin via non-covalent π-π interactions and yielded two conjugates: TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs. The physicochemical properties of the resultant conjugates were analysed using UV–Vis spectroscopy, Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Time-Correlated Single Proton Counting (TCSPC). The photocatalytic performance of the conjugates was evaluated by photodegradation of IBU in deionized (DI) under visible light illumination and a possible mechanism for the dominant active species and degradation pathway of IBU are also proposed.
Materials, equipment and photochemical and photophysical parameters items employed in this work are provided in the supporting information.

The synthesis of 5,10,15, 20-tetrakis(4-bromophenyl) porphyrin (Fig. 1 TBrP) [23] and indium (III) 5,10,15, 20-tetrakis(4-bromophenyl) porphyrin chloride (Fig. 1 InTBrP) [24] has been previously reported. We confirmed the structures of the porphyrins using mass spectra (see S1 and S2).

The intense colour of porphyrins is due to their highly conjugated π-electron systems. They have astonishing UV–Visible absorption spectra characteristics. UV–visible absorption spectrum is one of the most informative spectroscopic methods in the context of porphyrin chemistry [24]. The absorption spectrum of porphyrins has long been understood in terms of the highly successful “four-orbital” (two highest occupied π orbitals and two lowest unoccupied π* orbitals) proposed by Martin Gouterman [25,26].

According to Gouterman theory, the absorption bands systems arise from transitions between two highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) and two lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs), and it is the identities of the central metal, and the substituents affect the relative energies of these transitions. The HOMOs are a1u and an a2u orbital, while the LUMOs are degenerate set of eg orbitals. Transitions between these orbitals gave rise to two excited states. Orbital mixing splits these two states in energy, creating a higher energy state with greater oscillator strength, giving rise to the Soret band, and a lower energy state with less oscillator strength, giving rise to the Q-bands as seen in Fig. 2 .

The soret band for TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs in DMF were observed at 420 nm and 430 nm respectively. The second region consists of a weak transition to the first excited state in the range between 500 and 750 nm (the Q bands). These bands were observed at 515, 551, 599 and 649 nm for TBrP-GQDs, while those for InTBrP-GQDs were observed at 565 nm and 607 nm. Upon metalation of TBrP-GQDs the Soret band red shifted and the four Q bands merged into two. Introduction of heavy metal such as indium could result in a degree of perturbation and electron delocalisation within the porphyrin macrocycle [27], resulting in red-shift in absorption spectra.

Magnetic circular dichroism (MCD) spectroscopy is complementary to UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, as it can provide ground and excited state degeneracy information essential in understanding the electronic structure of molecules of high symmetry. MCD spectroscopy can be used to identify the main electronic Q(0,0) and B(0,0) bands of porphyrinoids, due to the presence of intense Faraday A1 terms or coupled pairs of oppositely-signed Faraday B0 terms [28,29].
DLS (Fig. 3 ) was used to determine the size of dispersed particles of GQDs. The hydrodynamic size determined for TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs were 15.3 nm and 20.4 nm respectively.
Equations and standards employed may be found in the Supporting Information.

Time-Correlated Single Proton Counting (TCSPC) was employed for τF studies. Fluorescence lifetimes (τF) can be defined as the average time a molecule spends in its singlet excited state before any other competing processes (i.e. fluorescence or intersystem crossing) can take place through photon emission to the ground state [30]. A typical fluorescence decay curve is depicted in Fig. 4 for InBrP-GQDs as an example. A mono-exponential fluorescence lifetime was observed.The τF were determined to be 2.6, 4.02 and 1.85 ns for InTBrP, TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs respectively in DMF, Table 1 .

We previously reported [23] the τF for TBrP at 5.08 ns. The values τF when H2BrP and InTBrP are conjugated to GQDs when excited at 490 nm increased and this could be due to the GQDs protecting the porphyrins. According to Ref. [31] the non-covalent interaction between the GQDs and porphyrin leads to omitting the zero-bandgap energy of the GQDs and therefore improving the fluorescence quantum yield.

The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) values are defined as the efficiency of the excited molecule to return to the electronic ground state by photon emission. This process depends on the electronic structure, steric and conformational interactions [32]. The ΦF for InTBrP, TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs were determined to be < 0.001, 0.16 and 0.01 respectively, Table 1. Otieno at el. reported the ΦF of InTBrP in toluene to be 0.006 [24].
Equations and standards employed may be found in the Supporting Information.

1O2 generation is one of the major factors which signifies the effectiveness of the photosensitizer since it is responsible for photodegradation of ECs [33]. ФΔ was studied under ambient conditions in DMF. The ФΔ were lower for the metal free porphyrins as compared to metalled ones due to the heavy atom effect of indium, which encourages intersystem crossing to the triplet state due to their size, therefore singlet oxygen production will be increased [34]. The highest ФΔ of 0.80 was that of InTBrP- GDQs as compared to the unmetalated conjugate, Table 1.

The photodegradation of IBU was carried out in a customized photoreactor by Lelesil Innovative Systems, India (250 W medium pressure mercury vapour lamp of quartz bulb used to irradiate UV radiation (200–400 nm at room temperature). The photodegradation efficiency of IBU using photocatalysts TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs over a period of 150 min is presented in Fig. 5 a. In the absence of the TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs, the concentration of IBU remained unchanged suggesting that IBU does not degrade due to self-photolysis. The un-metalated TBrP-GQDs photocatalyst had poor photodegrading capabilities of IBU (43.2%) compared to the improved 76.1% photodegradation efficiency of the InTBrP- GQDs. The notable increase in the photodegradation efficiency of the InTBrP-GQDs photocatalyst is due to the low electron-hole recombination rate and higher visible light absorption as well as the introduction of indium which promotes intersystem crossing to triple excited state due to the heavy atom effect [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of photocatalyst loading and initial IBU concentration
ce:section.ce:para: To establish the optimal InTBrP-GQDs concentration required to degrade IBU, the concentration was varied between 0.010 and 0.025 g/L−1 as depicted in Fig. 6 a. Maximum degradation was obtained when using 0.025 g/L−1 of the InTBrP-GQDs photocatalyst. Elsewhere, using a zinc porphyrin functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs/ZnPor) the optimal concentration required to degrade methylene blue was 0.3 μg/L−1 [12]. As the concentration of the InTBrP-GQDs increased, there was an initial increase in the photodegradation efficiency (kapp) until there was no further degradation observed, Table 2 . This trend is not surprising and has been reported previously by other researchers who attributed this phenomenon to light scattering [36,37]. To monitor the mineralization of IBU, the TOC (Total Organic Carbon) was measured during the photocatalytic reaction.

The TOC removal profiles of IBU using InTBrP-GQDs are presented in Fig. 6b, within 90 min of irradiation there was more than 70.4% of the TOC reduction. However, after 150 min there was a 93.2% TOC reduction. While this suggests that InTBrP-GQDs has great photocatalytic potential and if allowed longer reaction times could result in complete mineralization. It also shows that some IBU degradation by-products are formed albeit in quantities below the detection limit. Similar observations of incomplete mineralization after reaction time of IBU were cited in another study using TiO2/UV-LED system [38].

The selected concentration of the IBU used in this work was within the detection limit of the analytic technique. However, in real-life applications the concentration of IBU may differ significantly. To establish the correlation between the initial IBU concentration and the reaction rate, the concentration of IBU was varied between 0.010 and 0.025 g/L−1 while other variables were kept constant. From Fig. 7 and kinetic data presented in Table 2, a trend can be observed that increasing the IBU concentration initial increase in the photodegradation efficiency (kapp) until there was no further degradation observed. This is ascribed to the fact as the concentration of IBU increases, typically the generated photons and radicals will remain constant. This will result in the blockage of the active sites on InTBrP-GQDs or the reduction in the photons reaching the InTBrP-GQDs surface. At higher initial concentrations of IBU, number of photons entering the solution will be intercepted hence lower reaction rates. While at lower IBU concentrations there is an enhancement in the protons being absorbed.

The effect of pH on the photodegradation of IBU was studied at varying pH values (i.e., pH 3.0, pH 5.0, pH 7.0 and pH 9.0). The photodegradation of IBU was dependant on the pH (see Fig. 8 ). Notably at higher pH values, the photodegradation of IBU was not favoured and the k app decreased significantly. The recorded k app values were 0.00127, 0.00916, 0.00152 and 0.01024 at pH 3.0, 5.0, 7.0 and 9.0 respectively. The optimal pH for the photodegradation of IBU using InTBrP-GQDs was at pH 5.0. Poor photodegradation efficiencies of IBU at higher pH values (pH 7.0 and 9.0) is attributed to the enhanced electrostatic repulsion between the target IBU compounds and the InTBrP-GQDs resulting in the deterioration of adsorption capacity and photodegradation efficiency. Further, the CO2 that is generated during the mineralization of IBU when in an alkali environment can exist as HCO3 − (Equation (1)). In that case the generated HCO3 − will compete with the IBU for radicals [39] lowering the number of radicals being available to participate in the photodegradation of IBU which ultimately lowers the reaction rates.
(1)
H C
O 3 −
+ · O H → H C
O 3
· −
+
H 2
O

At pH 3.0 very little IBU was degraded, we speculate this is owing to IBU's inability to completely dissociate into its anionic form. Maximum IBU degradation was recorded at the optimum pH (pH 5). This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that when the pH is greater than the pKa value of IBU (pH 4.9), it will favour the formation of the dissociated species (IBU−) and enhancing the electrostatic interactions between the target IBU molecules and the InTBrP-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identifying radicals responsible for the degradation of IBU
ce:section.ce:para:
To establish the contribution and influence of the main active radicals involved in the degradation of IBU, scavenging experiments were carried out using 60 mM of methanol, Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA-2Na), p-benzoquinone and silver nitrate were used as scavengers for •OH, h+, •O2 − and e−, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9 , the addition of chemical scavengers in comparison to the no scavengers suppressed the degradation of IBU. The photolysis experiments confirmed that scavengers do not react with IBU. However, the contribution of the radical scavengers was in the following order •OH (93%) > •O2 − (79%) > e− (12%) h+ (8%). The low contribution of the h+ is because of h+ being consumed during the process of producing •OH free radicals [39]. Based on the results obtained for the singlet oxygen experiments Section 3.1.4 , it is not surprising that the second largest contributor to the degradation of IBU is •O2 −. The photocatalytic mechanisms are summarised in Equations (2)–(9). We postulate that when the two components in the InTBrP-GQDs photocatalyst formed from the H-type π-π stack the interactions get excited. The InTBrP will act as an electron donor, the photogenerated e− will likely get transferred to the conduction band of the GQDs. The molecular O2 will be reduced to form •O2 − by the e− in the InTBrP-GQDs, whereas the H2O will be oxidized to form •OH. Consequently, the IBU will be degraded predominantly by •O2 − and •OH.
(2)
I n T B r − G Q D s = h v →
e −
+
h +
(3)
O 2
+

→
∙ O
2 −
(4)
I B U +
∙ O

→
I B U
*
p r o d u c t s
(5)
H 2
O +
h +
→ ∙ O H +
H +
(6)
∙ O
2 −
+
H +
→
∙ H O
2
(7)
2 ∙ H
O 2
→
H 2
O 2
+
∙ O
2 −
(8)
∙ O
2 −
+
H 2
O 2
→ ∙ O H
(9)
I B U + ∙ O H →
I B U
*
p r o d u c t s

Using the photodegradation products obtained on LC-MS-QTOF and the effect of radical scavengers, a photodegradation pathway was proposed. The chromatograms in Fig. 10 were used to understand the evolution of the photodegradation products. The •OH and O2•- were identified as the main radicals responsible for the cleavage of the IBU molecule. While the InTBrP-GQDs was able to degrade the IBU, several intermediates including aromatic carboxylic and differently substituted phenolic compound were formed. A possible photodegradation mechanism was postulated and is depicted in Fig. 11 . In the presence of •OH and O2•- the IBU parent molecule formed Fragment A on Fig. 10 with total ion chromatogram of 222.0535 under negative ionization. Fragment A was matched/corresponds/identified as hydroxy-ibuprofen (molecular weight of 222.28 g mol−1). Other authors have reported on this intermediate previously [39]. The hydroxy-ibuprofen dissociated into Fragment B which was identified as 1-(4-ethyl-phenyl)-2-methyl-propan-1-ol (molecular weight of 178.27 g mol−1). Through ketonization, demethylation as well decarboxylation, the 1-(4-ethyl-phenyl)-2-methyl-propan-1-ol dissociated into as 4-ethyl-benzaldehyde (molecular weight 134.17 g mol−1) identified as 4-ethyl-benzaldehyde.

The stability and recyclability of the InTBrP-GQDs photocatalyst was tested. The reusability of materials is a crucial parameter in real-world applications. The InTBrP-GQDs photocatalyst used in the first cycle was tested over four successive cycles and data is shown in Fig. 12 .

It is interesting to note that during the first two cycles, the same amount of IBU was degraded with an efficiency above 76%. After the third and fourth cycle, while the photocatalyst remained stable there was a slight decrease in the photodegradation efficiency to 74% and 73%, respectively. This pattern is anticipated since there are fewer active sites available as the InTBrP-GQDs photocatalyst is recycled. Another reason for the slight decline (8%) in photodegradation efficiency is ascribed to the adsorption of intermediate products generated during the photocatalytic process, which may not be entirely desorbed. Other researchers have attributed the decline in photodegradation efficiencies to spallation of the photocatalyst, as seen in other heterostructure macromolecules using phthalocyanines [40]. The improved stability of the InTBrP-GQDs photocatalyst could also be attributed to the efficient separation of e−/h+ pairs brought about by the introduction of GQDs.

AOPs are driven by the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl and sulphate radicals [41]. Several activation sources are used for generating these species, which includes electrical energy, sound energy, solar energy or chemicals like ozone and hydrogen peroxide [42]. AOPs are thus classified into chemical, photochemical, sonochemical and electrochemical classes. Photochemical AOPs utilize light energy/UV radiation for generating ROS, making them a greener method. Moreover, they are comparably inexpensive and more effective than chemical AOPs [43]. Heterogenous photocatalysts are the most prominent nano-systems within the photochemical AOPs class [44–47]. They can be photolyzed with visible light at wavelengths from 400 to 800 nm, compared to photo peroxidation and photo ozonation that is photolyzed by ultraviolet radiation at less than 400 nm [42,43]. This suggests that natural sunlight can be utilized to photolyze heterogenous photocatalysts, making the study easily applicable in the real world. IBU can therefore be photodegraded using the photocatalysts presented in this report without the use of costly radiation equipment.

In summary, TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs photocatalysts were synthesized. The conjugates were formed through π-π stacking between GQDs and the porphyrins. The photophysical and photochemical studies of the complexes demonstrate that these nanoconjugates possesses favourable properties suitable for photodegradation applications. The Highest ФΔ was obtained for InTBrP- GDQs (ФΔ = 0.80), followed by InTBrP (ФΔ = 0.74). The photodegradation efficiency of the TBrP-GQDs and InTBrP-GQDs were determined to be 43.2 and 76.1% respectively. The photocatalytic reactions followed the Langmuir-Hinshelwood pseudo-first order kinetics. The photocatalytic activity of the InTBrP-GQDs was highly dependent on the pH and the optimal pH in the photodegradation of IBU using InTBrP-GQDs was at pH 5.0. Other parameters that influenced the photodegradation efficiency include the initial concentration of the IBU as well as the photocatalyst loading. Radical scavenging experiments showed that •OH and O2•- were the main radicals responsible for the degradation. The proposed degradation mechanism shows after 150 min through hydroxylation, ketonization, demethylation as well decarboxylation of the parent ibuprofen was degraded into smaller molecules with potential to eventually mineralize. The present work presents an alternate approach in the removal of IBU. Future work could possibly study the efficiency of the InTBrP-GQDs using real municipal water.

Bridged N. Magaela: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Knowledge S. Ndlovu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Charmaine S. Tshangana: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Adolph A. Muleja: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Bhekie B. Mamba: Conceptualization, Resources. Tebello Nyokong: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Muthumuni Managa: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper or any conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DSI/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University. Funding is also from the Institute for Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability (iNanoWS) at the College of Science, Engineering and Technology (CSET), University of South Africa.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2022.113147.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occurrence and potential risks of emerging contaminants in water
Visible Light Act. Struct. Photocatal. Remov. Emerg. Contam.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Lofrano, G. Libralato, S. Meric, V. Vaiano, O. Sacco, V. Venditto, M. Guida, M. Carotenuto, Occurrence and potential risks of emerging contaminants in water, in: Visible Light Act. Struct. Photocatal. Remov. Emerg. Contam., Elsevier, 2020: pp. 1-25. https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-818334-2.00001-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current advances in treatment technologies for removal of emerging contaminants from water – a critical review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.K. Shahid, A. Kashif, A. Fuwad, Y. Choi, Current advances in treatment technologies for removal of emerging contaminants from water - A critical review, Coord. Chem. Rev. 442 (2021) 213993. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccr.2021.213993.
